narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your journey toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to passing the bar exam as riskier than we'd like—landing at a 45% chance of failure that tipped the scales against admission. Picture your story as the model did: it started with your Year 3 grades placing you in the 4th decile at school, the single strongest signal pulling toward struggle on the bar, suggesting the demands of upper-level courses revealed challenges in keeping pace with peers. Layered on that was your Year 1 decile of 5th, reinforcing a pattern of mid-pack performance from the outset that made the model worry about building momentum. Your LSAT score of 28 echoed this, painting you as someone who'd need extra lift to master the intense reasoning the bar demands. As an Asian female from a middle-income family attending part-time while likely juggling work, these pieces together sketched a profile the model has seen falter before—your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 added to that cautious view, hinting at foundational hurdles in rigorous academics. Even your choice of part-time status, while practical, nudged the prediction downward, as the model links divided focus to tougher bar outcomes. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances, but this combination told a story of steady but not standout academic traction, especially later on, against the profiles that typically power through to bar success. The good news? This isn't set in stone—you could target those Year 3-style weaknesses now with focused prep, perhaps boosting test scores or GPA through targeted courses, to flip the script for future apps. You've got resilience in your story already; let's build on it. Hang in there.
